text:
Audio: Good morning, data 100. Welcome to another data 100 lecture. This is our first content lecture. Pretty exciting. So I'm going to talk to you about what we're going to see today. Please grab a seat. We cannot have anyone standing. We just want to make sure that we have the room capacity to comply with the fire cut. All right, cool. So the plan for today is, as I told you last time is that we're going to talk about pandas. But this pandas pandas is a programming library, Python programming library, that we're going to use a lot in data science and in data 100 and kind of every time that as a data scientist you try to code in Python you'll be using pandas. So let me fix the audio. All right, cool. So what we're going to do actually today and in the next couple of lectures is to immerse you in everything pandas. So we're going to start by talking about the data structures that we have in pandas, how to load the data, what are different data structures that you can load and play with the data. And then we're going to also start talking about how we can do data extraction. If you want to filter your data set so that you look at a bunch of features and only a limited number of rows, how do we do that? That's also something that we're going to talk about today and that's using lock, eye lock and selection operator that you're seeing in the agenda. Video: 1: Lec 02 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas |

text:
Audio: So let's dive right into things. So we talked about today and also in the previous lecture, we talked about data. We say that, okay, we're dealing with the data, were extracting the data, we're processing the data, but we didn't talk about what this data is. So talking about the data and where data comes in this data science lifecycle that we saw in the previous lecture, we've been over a great demo with a lot of different pandas and text. And you saw that we need pandas in order to be able to process loads of data and be able to do fun things, whether it be able to visualize the data, to filter the data and extract information from the data that we have. We saw that there are two entry points into this data science lifecycle. There's one thing that we ask a question. And then we go about finding the data or that the lifecycle starts with providing the data to you. What we're going to expect, I spent a lot of time in the first three weeks is the exploratory data analysis that we're, for short, we're calling it EDA. So in EDA, we're going to show you how you can load the data, how to process the data, how to visualize the data, how to process textual information, and also in addition to that, how to be able to visualize all the information that you have in the data set that you have. So this is the entire kind of, we're gonna spend the first three weeks of data 100 so that you know where we stand. But I basically talked about data a lot. I say that we're giving you the data, we're handing you the data, we are processing the data, but I don't talk about what this data is, other than this abstract idea of me throwing a box of data at you. So today, we're going to talk about what this data actually is, how is it stored, how can we load and process data in different formats, and what is the standard way for us to think about data? So we're going to think about the data as a tabular data. Video: 1: v

text:
Audio: So you can see that it starts from 1824 and then goes all the way to the 2020 election. So this is the data set that we're working with. But as data scientists, we have a contract that when we're thinking about a tabular data in 2D format, we're always referring to rows as a single observation or a single sample or a single individual in this data set that we had dealt with. So for example, in this case, this single row corresponds to John Adams, who ran for presidents in 1824 or the time that the 1824 year that they ran for presidents, if there's someone who ran multiple times or over multiple different years. So we think about Rose as each one of those individual observations or a sample, we're going to keep talking about the resolution of the or the granularity of our data set in features lectures, but for today you just assume that whatever the granularity or the resolution of your data is, you're going to refer to one single data point as one of the rows in your data. And obviously the columns are going to be attributes or different features of those of those data points that you're holding. So for example here, this is the feature related to party. So all of those individuals do have party affiliation or if there is data missing you will see that that feature is no That's something that you will see in homeworks and labs and we're gonna take a look at that later on and let's see how we can handle Missing values, but for this is specific case all of the data The entire data set is filled in with all of the possible values And then what you're seeing is that party is a feature or is an attribute? So we're going to talk about feature, attribute, or characteristic of those individuals. So what are different features that we have in this data set? We have a feature for a year, we have a feature for the candidate name, we have a feature for party affiliation and so on and so forth. Video: 1: v

text:
Audio: And you'll keep seeing that in handouts and in different places. And you're also seeing that on the website as well. So let's get into what pandas can do. So I say pandas is a programming library. So it's a programming library that gives you a lot of functionality. And starting functionality that we want is basically to be able to arrange data in a tab alert format. There are different data structures that are supported by pandas, how we can load the data, how we can take a look at the data. It will tell us how we can extract useful information from the data. So, for example, if you have this election dataset, and if you want to extract, for example, what is the maximum popular percentage of votes that a candidate got? You can basically use Pandas functionality in order to search through your data frame and be able to extract that information. You can gain insights, you can visualize it, you can filter it, you can do all fancy stuff in order to process your data and gain more information. You can do feature engineering. So for example, say that you have the popular percentage, right? If you want to change that feature into a different feature that is more useful for the modeling tasks that you will do down the road, remember that we're in the third data science lifecycle, the fourth data science lifecycle stage is going to be modeling. text:
Audio: So we'll keep doing everything possible to do the modeling to make the modeling phase as easy as possible. So we might want to do feature engineering. Feature engineering is that, okay, I have these all of these percentages, but what if I want to turn those percentages into ratio, ratio over like the, the highest percentage of votes that it can't get at God in that specific election right that's an example of transforming the data uh... in terms of doing transformation there are a lot of times that we can use numpy so we're using numpy so that we're not implementing all of those uh... mathematical calculations ourselves and another Kind of benefit of pandas is that it will perform all of those operations in a vectorized way. So remember that we had columns as features so it will turn one specific column into one long vector or one very large vector. And then all of the calculation that we're doing is going to be vector calculation, vector multiplication, vector addition and different different kind of operations that we run on vectors. so that the processing can be paralyzed and then the calculation or training of models can be easier. That's the power of... that transforming data into a table or format so that using that table or format your data can be represented as vectors then the computation can be much easier kind of making a rational about like why we do certain things and why we're not loading data in any other way. In data 100 so you can think about this lecture and the next two lectures as kind of for those those who know data science library. So pandas is kind of industry standard and academia standard for doing data science kind of operations. Audio: You can click on the lecture 2 and then be able to get the code and run it and whatnot. So. Now let's dive into starting to know what are those data structures? How can we create them? How can we use them? I'm going to start with data frame because we said that tables are data frames in pandas language. And this table is this this to the structure. It has a bunch of columns. It has a bunch of rows. One thing that I want you to note is that each one of those rows they have a label that is kind of noted by a bold color or both letters. And then we also have labels for the columns, or these are the names for the columns, that are also labels that are also showing what attribute or what's the name of that attribute in each one of those respective columns. The way that I want you to think about this 2D structure is that it is a collection of columns. So we have a bunch of columns that we aggregate it together in order to build a data structure called data frame. and each one of those columns is a series object. series is another data structure in pandas. So series is a one D structure. It's a sequence of values. So you can think about, for example, if I look at the candidate, candidate by itself, that single column is a series object. Video: 1: v

Audio: So if I go ahead and here, for example, I say that, OK, s dot index, it will tell me that, oh, you didn't provide your index, but I went ahead and create a range object or a range index object. I started the range index at zero. I went ahead and created and tacked each one of these rows with these numerical values up to 3 and not including 3 that's how the range object works. And then it will tell me that this step. value is equal to one because it will just is that it is included by one every time. Question. Okay, there's a good question that whether the advantage of a serious object versus a data frame. So there's a lot of time that when you're doing the modeling, the input to each one of those training or those optimizers has to be a data frame. And there are different ways that we will say that quickly we can transform a 1D series into a data frame and vice versa. But there is no clear other advantage other than like if you're also printing. Serious object is not going to be printed nicely so you're seeing that this is not table like versus if you want it to be table like you might want to turn it into a data frame and use it there. So most of the time, it is not numerical value and it's objects. text:
Audio: So this is kind of a wrap-up of series object. So then the next thing that we want to talk about is how can we combine all of these series objects in order to create a data frame. So in order to create a data frame, you think about having a series object with the index, those index values that we had in the election, they were automatically created, they're starting from 0 all the way to 181. And then the name of the first column or the first series was here, I'll keep combining with the candidate series, I'll keep combining it with all of the other columns that we have all the way to the point that we create this 2D structure. What I want you to notice is that each one of these series object, each one of them actually had... And index right and these index values were the same or this index object or the values in that index object were shared between all of those series objects and that's why we could combine them together after we combine all of those columns together now we have that same index. refer to as the index of this entire data frame so that we can distinguish between different rows using those labels. And then what you're seeing is that for each one of these columns that we had, we had the name. So the name is going to appear as the column label. So the first series object had the name year or the name of that series object was year. So the label for the first column is going to be here. the label, the name for the second column is candidate, so the label for the second column is going to be candidate, so on and so forth. text:
Audio: And that's how data frame is created. So again, think about data frame as this 2D structure. It has two sets of labels. It has labels for the columns. It has labels for the rows, and then we'll work with those labels in order to get access to individual elements. So with that simple explanation and now I want to go ahead and create data frame. So creating data frame is going to be. It can be can be happening in different ways there are different syntax and you don't actually need to memorize all of them and in practice You're not going to manually create data frame a lot most of the time we're gonna give you a CSV file or spreadsheet You will load that automatically so you're not manually creating it, but it's good to know how I can be created So, data frame also has a constructor data frame, right? It also has a bunch of arguments that we can pass. The arguments that we pass, the first one is data exactly similar to series. The second one is index. Again, similar to the series, it's not a required argument or parameter that you need to pass because if you don't provide it, it will automatically label the rows or create the index object using the RENGE index starting from zero all the way. And then here you're also providing columns, which columns do you want to create or whether they're labels for your columns. If your data does not have labels, and then this way you can provide labels or names for each one of those columns so that your data frame is going to be more readable for you. There are different ways to create it, as I said. The most useful one and the one that you will see most is going to be using a CSV file. Video: 1: v

But here you're seeing that in kind of a table format. What if I have multiple columns? If I have multiple columns, I need to move beyond a one-dimensional list and go into two-dimensional lists. So you think about what the list that we had here, right? So the list that I had here, each this outer list, was basically going over different rows. Each one of those elements, they were referring to different rows that I had. Here is also the same thing. The outer kind of list is going over rows and the inner list, which I have here, is each one of those Actually the... The other way around. So that was the first column and then here I have um each one of those rows. So you're referring to if you're taking a look at one and one, here is one row, and then here this is also row two, and then what you're seeing is here that I'm collecting, all of them stacking them over into that column. So every time you can set it, you can reset it, you can change your mind. There is flexibility into how to use Index object in a data frame. We said that index values does not need to be unique. They can be numeric They can be non numeric, but for column names, we usually want them to be unique And we just want to make sure that if we have an attribute for candidate or refers to the candidate name We don't have to candidate column and there might be a chance that the values corresponding to a certain row in those different columns are going to be different and that can be problematic, right? It will remember where it was in the sequence of those columns that it had. And then it will automatically create all of these. Automatic in the indices that I had from before, right? So every time you can set it, you can reset it, you can change your mind. There is flexibility into how to use Index object in a data frame. We said that index values does not need to be unique. They can be numeric They can be non numeric, but for column names, we usually want them to be unique And we just want to make sure that if we have an attribute for candidate or refers to the candidate name We don't have to candidate column and there might be a chance that the values corresponding to a certain row in those different columns are going to be different and that can be problematic, right? So a lot of times we we want the column names to be unique so that a single source of information is available in each one of the given columns that we have. So, we... I have seen that this index how we can create a data frame, how we can set the index.

Audio: Bye. 4 Three. 2 and 1. So the next piece I promise you is interesting, but again, because you know the logic is going to be very quick and very easy. So we're going to talk about how we can do data extraction. So we're going to basically be able to do data extraction in order to select a number of columns, a number of rows, and then be able to just take a look at a snapshot of a data frame or a serious object that you have. There are different ways, different common ways for us to extract the data. There's one way that we can, once I hand you a data, you can take a look at the first few rows or the last few rows. So looking at the top rows or the bottom rows is the usual thing that you would do in order to make sense of, OK, what are the column labels, whether those values looking like, do I need to do any data cleaning or whatnot? We'll keep using it a lot, just to take a look at the top part or the bottom part of the data set. There are different ways to do data extraction using labels. So you might be asking why did I bother about labels a lot because they can be used for data extraction. We might want to also use integer positions in order to figure out how to extract the data. That's also another way that we can extract the data. But take a look at the first piece. Just taking a look at the top part of the data frame or the bottom part of the part of the data frame, we can use .head or .tail in order to get take a look at the top part or the bottom part of the of the data frame. Audio: If we provide this end as an argument, it will get you n number of rows. Whether that be the top rows or the bottom rows, it will get you n number of rows. If we don't provide that number, the default value is going to be the default n is equal to five. We're always taking a look at the top five. So for example, in this case, if I have elections, if I go ahead and say dot head five, or just say dot head, it will give me the top five rows. If I take a look at dot tail five or dot tail, it will give me the last five rows. and I should be able to just generally take a look at, okay, does it have labels, what is index, what is column labels, how does the data look like? That's a normal thing that we will always do when we have a new data set in our pile. Using label-based extraction. Remember, I said I have column labels, I have row labels, and by this time you should be tired of me keep repeating that labels are very important. They are very important because we can locate a part of a data frame using those labels. So the function that we'll do that for us is called the lock. This is an operator that uses a score brackets. it will get a number of row labels and it will get a number of column labels and it will be able to extract or give us the numbers those rows or those columns at that specific intersection for us. So lock this L refers to label-based extraction. We're doing extraction based on the label. Video: 1: v

text:
Audio: So let's take a look at that together. So if I say that I'm providing a list of labels for the rows. Here's label 87, 25 and 179. It will give me exactly those rows, 87, 25 and 179. Right? And then the list for the columns here, what you're seeing is that I'm asking only for the year column candidate column and result column and I'm only getting those values. So it will basically indexing filtering for the columns, filtering for the rows and only looking at that intersection. Notice that the first argument here is row labels, second argument is column labels. So row labels is always important. You have to always provide row labels in whatever format that you want. But the column labels are optional. If you don't provide column labels, it will give you all of the columns. It mostly assumes that you're filtering under rows, but you're not necessarily filtering under columns. What happens if you have mismatch ordering or if you provide only one list, but that list is your column list. is your column list instead of rule list, it will give you key error. Key error for you who know dictionary is that it says that, oh, I can't find this key in this dictionary is structured.
